title: Tandoori chicken
subtitle: Marinated chicken legs grilled until the surface chars
excerpt: Chicken leg portions marinated overnight in a spiced yoghurt mixture with garlic, ginger, and whole ground spices, then grilled at maximum heat until the skin is charred and the marinade has caramelised into the surface. The bone-in leg gives the dish its distinctive char-edged silhouette. A Punjabi original and the dish that introduced the tandoor tradition to British Indian restaurants.
sourceNotes: Tandoori chicken was developed by Kundan Lal Gujral at Moti Mahal restaurant in Peshawar (now Pakistan) in the 1940s, and brought to Delhi after Partition. It arrived in Britain in the 1960s and was one of the first Indian restaurant dishes to gain broad public recognition.

content.text: Tandoori chicken depends on two things: the marinade penetrating the meat, and the cooking temperature being high enough to char. The first is addressed by making deep slashes through the skin into the flesh (three or four cuts per piece) which allows the yoghurt spice mixture to reach the interior. Without the slashes, the marinade flavours only the surface.

At home, the oven grill is the best approximation of the tandoor's radiant heat. Position the rack as high as possible, preheat the grill on its maximum setting for 10 minutes, and do not open the oven door unnecessarily during cooking. The goal is surface charring while the interior cooks through, bone-in chicken legs take 25 to 30 minutes at maximum grill heat.

items.name: Chicken legs
items.prepNote: skin-on, slash deeply 3 to 4 times per piece

content.text: Slash the {{chicken-legs}} deeply through the skin and flesh, 3 to 4 cuts per piece, so the marinade reaches the interior. Combine the {{plain-yoghurt}}, {{garlic}}, {{ginger-root}}, lemon juice, {{chilli-powder}}, {{garam-masala}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{coriander-ground}}, {{turmeric}}, {{vegetable-oil}}, and salt. Rub the marinade thoroughly into the chicken pieces, pushing it into the slashes. Cover and refrigerate for at least 8 hours, or overnight.

Bring the chicken to room temperature for 30 minutes before cooking. Preheat the grill to its highest setting with the rack in the highest position. Lay the chicken on a foil-lined baking tray. Grill for 15 minutes, then turn and grill for a further 12 to 15 minutes until the surface is charred in patches and the internal temperature reaches 74°C at the thickest point. Rest for 5 minutes before serving. Serve with lemon wedges, sliced raw onion, and mint raita.

items.fix: Wipe off excess marinade before grilling, a thick wet coating steams rather than chars. Preheat the grill for the full 10 minutes before the chicken goes in
items.cause: Grill not hot enough or marinade too thick

items.fix: The leg joint is the last part to cook. Push a skewer into the thickest part near the bone, the juices should run clear. Continue for 5-minute intervals until done
Move the rack one position lower and increase total cook time by 5 to 8 minutes. The lower position allows the heat to penetrate without burning the outside
items.cause: Rack too close to the heat element

items.fix: Pat the chicken dry with paper before applying the marinade. A damp surface prevents the yoghurt adhering to the skin
items.symptom: Marinade washes off and doesn't adhere

content.text: Tandoori chicken is one of the dishes that defined the British Indian restaurant from the 1960s onward. The red-orange charred leg on a cast-iron sizzle plate became the signature image of the curry house. The dish is closely linked to the Moti Mahal restaurants in Delhi and their use of the traditional clay tandoor oven. A tandoor runs at 400 to 500°C, which no home oven can match. The home version, using a maximum-heat grill, gives a real result: charred, smoky, with the marinade caramelised onto the surface.
